distribution_analysis.most_covered_section: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
questions.question: What is the solution set for the inequality \(7x + 3 < 5x + 9\)?
questions.hint: Isolate the variable \(x\) by subtracting \(5x\) and \(3\) from both sides.
questions.explanation: Solving \(7x + 3 < 5x + 9\) yields \(2x < 6\) which simplifies to \(x < 3\), representing all numbers less than 3. Option C (x<1) incorrectly divides by 2 too early.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities

explanations_tiered.definition: A linear inequality in one variable is a statement comparing two expressions using <, >, ≤, or ≥.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Solving inequalities uses similar steps to equations, but multiplying/dividing by a negative reverses the sign.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a seesaw: if \(7x+3\) is lighter than \(5x+9\), moving numbers around keeps the same direction until you divide.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Isolate \(x\) by subtracting \(5x\) and \(3\) from both sides, giving \(2x<6\). Dividing by 2 yields \(x<3\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The solution \(x<3\) defines an open interval \((-\infty,3)\). Dividing by a positive (2) does not flip the inequality sign.
misconceptions: Some believe dividing by 2 reverses the inequality, which is false for positive divisors.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Inequality: isolate variable; reverse sign only when multiplying/dividing by negative.

glossary_entry.definition: An inequality involving a linear expression.

flashcard.front: What is the solution set for \(7x+3<5x+9\)?
flashcard.back: \((-\infty, 3)\). Subtract \(5x\) and 3 to get \(2x<6\), then divide by 2.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Subtract \(5x\) and 3 to get \(2x<6\), then divide by 2: \(x<3\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A mathematical statement comparing two linear expressions using inequality symbols.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often forget to isolate variable fully. Emphasize step-by-step.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve linear inequalities in one variable

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
questions.question: Why does multiplying both sides of an inequality by a negative number reverse the inequality sign?

options.A: Because the number line is symmetric about zero
options.B: Because negative numbers are less than positive numbers
options.C: Because the order of numbers is reversed on the number line when multiplied by a negative
options.D: Because multiplication by a negative is not allowed in inequalities

questions.hint: Consider multiplying \(2 < 5\) by \(-1\). What happens to the order?
questions.explanation: Multiplying by a negative reflects numbers across zero on the number line, reversing their order. Option B is vague; the key is the reversal of positions relative to zero.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities

explanations_tiered.definition: The reversal property states that if \(a < b\) and \(c < 0\), then \(ac > bc\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Multiplying by a negative flips the positions of numbers relative to zero on the number line.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of \(2<5\). Multiply by \(-1\): \(-2 > -5\). The smaller number becomes larger after flipping sides.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Negative multiplication reverses order because numbers are reflected across zero: \(-2\) is to the right of \(-5\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Formally, \(a<b\) implies \(a-b<0\). Multiplying by \(c<0\) gives \(c(a-b)>0\), so \(ac>bc\).
misconceptions: Some think multiplication by a negative just changes signs but keeps inequality direction.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Multiplying/dividing by negative: flip < to > and vice versa.

glossary_entry.definition: Multiplying an inequality by a negative number reverses the inequality symbol.

flashcard.front: What happens to the inequality sign when multiplying by a negative?
flashcard.back: It reverses direction because the order on the number line is flipped.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does multiplying an inequality by a negative reverse the sign?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It reflects numbers across zero on the number line, reversing order.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The rule that multiplying an inequality by a negative flips the inequality symbol.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a number line to visually demonstrate why -2 > -5 when 2 < 5.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Multiplying by negative reverses order on the number line.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
questions.question: A student has Birr 120 to buy exercise books (Birr 20 each) and pens (Birr 10 each). Which inequality represents the possible numbers of books \(x\) and pens \(y\)?
questions.hint: The total amount spent cannot exceed the available Birr 120.
questions.explanation: The total cost \(20x + 10y\) must be less than or equal to the budget of 120. Option A represents spending at least 120, which is not required.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities

explanations_tiered.definition: A budget constraint limits total spending to at most the available money.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The sum of costs must be ≤ the budget. Equality is allowed only if exactly all money is spent.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you have 120 Birr, you cannot spend more than that. So total cost must be ≤ 120.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Each book costs 20, each pen costs 10, so \(20x+10y \le 120\) keeps spending within budget.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The inequality includes the boundary \(20x+10y=120\) where the budget is fully used, and the interior where money remains.
reasoning_steps: Identify cost per item: book=20, pen=10
misconceptions: Some use = instead of ≤, forgetting leftover money is allowed.

glossary_entry.definition: An inequality limiting spending to available funds.

flashcard.front: A student has Birr 120. Books cost 20, pens cost 10. Write the budget inequality.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Budget constraint for books (20) and pens (10) with 120 Birr?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Limits total expenditure to available funds.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize difference between ≤ and < ; ≤ includes equality.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Translate word problems into linear inequalities

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
questions.question: A taxi charges a flat rate of Birr 1.75 plus Birr 0.75 per km. A person incorrectly writes \(0.75x + 1.75 = 10\) instead of an inequality. What is the consequence of this error?

options.A: It assumes the total cost is exactly 10 Birr, ignoring the possibility of spending less
options.B: It assumes the taxi ride is free
options.C: It calculates the cost per km incorrectly
options.D: It assumes the flat rate changes with distance

questions.hint: The phrase 'at most Birr 10' means the cost can be less than or equal to 10.
questions.explanation: Using equality \(=10\) forces the cost to be exactly 10, but the passenger may spend less. The correct inequality is \(0.75x+1.75 \le 10\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities

explanations_tiered.definition: A misconception is using an equation when an inequality is required.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The condition 'at most' implies ≤, not =, because cost can be less than the maximum.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you have 10 Birr, you can spend 10 or less, not exactly 10 every time.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The expression \(0.75x+1.75\) must be ≤ 10; setting it equal to 10 forces the passenger to spend all money, which is not required.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The inequality \(0.75x+1.75 \le 10\) includes a continuum of feasible distances, whereas the equation gives only one specific distance.
misconceptions: Using = when the problem allows any value up to a limit.

glossary_entry.definition: An incorrect belief that using = satisfies all budget constraints.

flashcard.front: Why is \(0.75x+1.75 = 10\) wrong for 'at most 10 Birr'?
flashcard.back: It forces exact spending; ≤ allows spending less or equal.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What error occurs when using = for 'at most 10 Birr'?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It forces exact spending, ignoring that ≤ allows less.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Using equality when inequality is required.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Highlight the difference between exact equality and inequality in word problems.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Translate word problems into inequalities correctly
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Equality ignores the possibility of spending less than 10.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
questions.subsection: 4.1.3 Linear Inequalities in Two Variables and Their Graphical Solutions
questions.question: What is the name for the region on one side of a line in the \(xy\)-plane?
questions.hint: The line divides the plane into two opposite regions.
questions.explanation: A line splits the plane into two half-planes. Quadrants are formed by two perpendicular lines, not just one line.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 4.1.3 Linear Inequalities in Two Variables and Their Graphical Solutions

explanations_tiered.definition: A half-plane is the set of all points on one side of a line in the coordinate plane.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A line acts as a boundary dividing the plane into two half-planes.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a line drawn on a piece of paper. Each side of the line is a half-plane.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The line \(y = mx + c\) splits the plane into upper and lower half-planes.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Formally, a half-plane is a set \(\{(x,y) | ax+by > c\}\) or \(< c\), with the line \(ax+by=c\) as boundary.
misconceptions: Confusing half-plane with quadrant (quadrant requires two axes).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Half-plane: one side of a line.

glossary_entry.definition: Region on one side of a line.

flashcard.back: The region on one side of a line in the \(xy\)-plane.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Region on one side of a line in the coordinate plane.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Set of points on one side of a line.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Half-plane is the correct term.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
questions.subsection: 4.1.3 Linear Inequalities in Two Variables and Their Graphical Solutions
questions.structural_anchor: steps to draw the graph of inequalities
questions.question: When graphing \(y < 5x + 7\), why must the line \(y = 5x + 7\) be drawn as a dashed line?

options.B: Because points on the line are not included in the solution set

questions.hint: Strict inequalities do not include the boundary.
questions.explanation: The inequality \(y < 5x + 7\) is strict, so points on the line are excluded, indicated by a dashed line. Option B is correct but less precise; the direct reason is the strict inequality symbol.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 4.1.3 Linear Inequalities in Two Variables and Their Graphical Solutions
source_trace.structural_element: Steps to draw the graph of inequalities

explanations_tiered.definition: A strict inequality (< or >) excludes points on the boundary line.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Dashed lines indicate that boundary points are not part of the solution region.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you can't include the line itself, you draw it as dashes to show it's not part of the answer.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For \(y < 5x+7\), the line is a boundary that does not satisfy the inequality, so it is dashed.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The inequality \(y < 5x+7\) defines an open half-plane; the boundary \(y = 5x+7\) is excluded, hence dashed.
misconceptions: Some use dashed line for ≤ or ≥ incorrectly.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: < or > → dashed line. ≤ or ≥ → solid line.

flashcard.front: When do you use a dashed line for graphing an inequality?
flashcard.back: When the inequality is strict (< or >), not including the boundary.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Inequality that does not include equality.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that dashed line means boundary not part of solution.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Graph linear inequalities in two variables
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Strict inequality < requires dashed line.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
questions.subsection: 4.1.4 Systems of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables and Graphical Solutions
questions.question: What is the graphical solution of a system of linear inequalities called?
questions.hint: It is the set of all points satisfying all inequalities simultaneously.
questions.explanation: The feasible region is the intersection of all half-planes defined by the inequalities. It represents all possible solutions to the system.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 4.1.4 Systems of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables and Graphical Solutions

explanations_tiered.definition: The feasible region is the set of all points satisfying every inequality in a system.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Graphically, it's the overlapped shaded area where all constraints hold.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Each inequality gives a shaded area. The overlap of all shaded areas is the feasible region.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For a system, the feasible region is the intersection of all half-planes.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The feasible region may be bounded (polygon) or unbounded; it contains all potential solutions.
misconceptions: Confusing feasible region with a single half-plane.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Feasible region = overlap of all inequality graphs.

glossary_entry.definition: Set of all points satisfying all constraints.

flashcard.back: Graphical solution of a system of inequalities; the overlap of all half-planes.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The set of all points satisfying all inequalities in a system.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Intersection of all half-planes from constraints.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show with multiple inequalities how overlap forms the feasible region.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Feasible region is the graph of all solutions.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
questions.subsection: 4.1.4 Systems of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables and Graphical Solutions
questions.structural_anchor: How to Solve a System of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables
questions.question: When solving a system graphically, after drawing the boundary lines, what is the next step?

options.A: Calculate the slope of each line
options.B: Test a point in one half-plane to determine which side to shade
options.C: Find the intersection of the lines

questions.hint: You need to decide which side of each boundary satisfies the inequality.
questions.explanation: Testing a point (like (0,0)) shows which half-plane satisfies the inequality. Then shade that side for each inequality before finding overlap.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 4.1.4 Systems of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables and Graphical Solutions
source_trace.structural_element: How to Solve a System of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables

explanations_tiered.definition: Testing a point determines which side of a boundary line satisfies the inequality.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If the test point satisfies the inequality, shade that side; otherwise shade the opposite.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Pick a point not on the line. If it works, shade that side; if not, shade the other side.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using (0,0) is common; if it satisfies \(y \le 2x+3\), shade the region containing (0,0).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The test point method relies on the fact that a line divides the plane into two half-planes where the inequality holds on exactly one side (if the line itself is excluded).
misconceptions: Shading without testing, leading to wrong side.

glossary_entry.definition: Method to determine which half-plane satisfies an inequality.

flashcard.front: After drawing a boundary line for an inequality, what should you do next?
flashcard.back: Test a point to see which side to shade.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the second step after drawing boundary lines for a system of inequalities?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Test a point to determine which side of each line to shade.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Using a point to decide shading side.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Always recommend using (0,0) if not on boundary.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Testing a point is the next logical step.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
questions.subsection: 4.1.4 Systems of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables and Graphical Solutions
questions.structural_anchor: How to Solve a System of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables
questions.question: Why is the feasible region for a system of inequalities usually a polygon or an unbounded area?

options.A: Because each inequality contributes a straight-line boundary
options.C: Because the intersection of half-planes creates polygonal shapes
options.D: Because the solution must be a convex set

questions.hint: Each inequality defines a half-plane; their intersection forms a convex region.
questions.explanation: Each linear inequality defines a half-plane bounded by a line. The intersection of several half-planes yields a convex polygon (or unbounded convex region).

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 4.1.4 Systems of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables and Graphical Solutions
source_trace.structural_element: How to Solve a System of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables

explanations_tiered.definition: The intersection of half-planes is a convex polygon or unbounded convex region.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Each half-plane is convex; intersection of convex sets is convex, with linear boundaries forming a polygon.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Each inequality cuts the plane with a straight line. The overlap of all cuts creates a shape with straight edges.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Because each inequality's boundary is a line, the intersection region is bounded by segments of those lines, forming a polygon.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Convexity follows from the fact that each half-plane is convex and intersection preserves convexity; linear boundaries produce polygonal edges.
reasoning_steps: Intersection of convex sets is convex
misconceptions: Thinking feasible region could be curved; linear constraints give only straight boundaries.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Intersection of half-planes = convex polygon or unbounded convex region.

glossary_entry.definition: Region where any line segment between two points lies entirely inside.

flashcard.front: Why is the feasible region for linear inequalities a polygon or unbounded area?
flashcard.back: Intersection of half-planes bounded by lines yields convex polygonal shapes.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What shape is the feasible region for linear inequalities?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Convex polygon or unbounded convex region formed by intersecting half-planes.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Region where line segment between any two points stays inside.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Feasible region = intersection of half-planes → convex polygon.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use examples of bounded (triangle) and unbounded (half-plane with extra constraints) regions.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Intersection of half-planes yields polygonal shapes.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
questions.subsection: 4.1.5 Further on the System of Inequalities
questions.question: What is the name for the intersection point of two boundary lines of a solution region?
questions.hint: It is a corner point where constraints meet.
questions.explanation: A vertex (or corner point) is where two boundary lines intersect. These points are critical for optimization in linear programming.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 4.1.5 Further on the System of Inequalities

explanations_tiered.definition: A vertex is a point where two boundary lines of a solution region intersect.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Vertices are corner points of the feasible region, often where the optimal solution occurs.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a polygon's corners — those are vertices. Each vertex is where two sides meet.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In linear programming, vertices are intersections of constraint boundaries and are candidates for optimal solutions.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Fundamental Theorem of Linear Programming states that if an optimal solution exists, it occurs at a vertex of the feasible region.
reasoning_steps: Find intersection point of two lines
misconceptions: Calling any interior point a vertex; vertices are only at intersections of boundaries.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Vertex = intersection of two boundary lines = corner point.

glossary_entry.definition: Intersection point of two boundary lines of a feasible region.

flashcard.front: What is a vertex in linear programming?
flashcard.back: Intersection point of two boundary lines of the feasible region.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Define vertex of a feasible region.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Intersection point of two boundary lines.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Corner point formed by intersecting boundaries.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 4.1.5 Further on the System of Inequalities
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Vertex is the correct term for intersection of boundary lines.
